Thanks!
I keep reading `moveBefore()` wrong.
It's lost all meaning to me I've read it all so much now.
wrong in the sense of what the receiver/arguments are?
cause that confounds me every time
Yeah logically based on name I'd expect the caller to be the thing that moves.
Yea it was supposed to be consistent with `insertBefore` with the thought that we would add move semantics to the other verbs in the future
content.formatted_body: Yea it was supposed to be consistent with insertBefore with the thought that we would add move semantics to the other verbs in the future

content.body: But the DOM APIs all follow this weirdness so consistency is probably best
my attempts to sneak a preference for smalltalk function naming conventions into the TAG design principles failed. ;-)

(in particular, I think smalltalk has a practice where function names are actually split up and the arguments placed in between the parts of the name, so a function called moveBefore would clearly have the thing you move as the first argument and the thing it's before as the second argument

* (in particular, I think smalltalk has a practice where function names are actually split up and the arguments placed in between the parts of the name, so a function called moveBefore would clearly have the thing you move as the first argument and the thing it's before as the second argument.)

content.body: Maybe adding `moveReplaceChildren`, `moveAppend`, `movePrepend`, `moveReplaceWith` will help here

yeah, I guess this does follow those principles.  I never remember, I just have to look it up if I want to use insertBefore or moveBefore.  Or I can use Element.before() or Element.after() without looking it up.

Consider the sentence (in HTML 4.11.3.5) "The Label of the command is the value of the option element's label attribute, if there is one, or else the option element's descendant text content, with ASCII whitespace stripped and collapsed." which I'll abbreviate as "X is A, or else B, with C." So, on the face of it, there's an ambiguity as to whether the "with C" applies to "A or B", or just "B". Is there a rule that tells me which is the intended/correct parse?
I don't know that there's a good general rule -- but in this case I think it's probably just B.
the spec could probably use a `<dl switch>` here.
content.formatted_body: the spec could probably use a <dl switch> here.

content.body:
```
<dt>If the option element has a label attribute
<dd>the value of that attribute
<dt>otherwise
<dd>the option element's descendant text content, with ASCII whitespace stripped and collapsed
```
